elements.sent-2411-001: In this paper, we will provide a result on the relativistic Euler equations for an ideal gas equation of state and a physical vacuum boundary.
elements.sent-2411-002: More specifically, we will prove a priori estimates for the linearized system in weighted Sobolev spaces.
elements.sent-2411-003: Our focus will be on choosing the correct thermodynamic variables, developing a weighted book-keeping scheme, and then proving energy estimates for the linearized system.
elements.sent-2411-004: Keywords: Relativistic Euler equations, physical vacuum boundary, ideal gas equation of state.
elements.sent-2411-006: The starting point for our work is the relativistic Euler equations which describe the motion of a relativistic fluid in a Minkowski background (see \cite{disconzi2023recent for historical context).}
elements.sent-2411-007: The equations of motion are given by \begin{subequations { \begin{equation}u^\mu \partial_\mu \varrho + (p+\varrho) \partial_\mu u^\mu = 0, \label{E:Energy_conservation} (p+\varrho) u^\mu \partial_\mu u^\alpha + \proj^{\alpha\mu} \partial_\mu p =0, \label{E:Momentum_conservation} g_{\mu\nu} u^\mu u^\nu = -1, \label{E:Constraint_four_velocity}\end{equation} \end{subequations where $\varrho$ is the fluid's (energy) density, $u$ is the fluid's (four-)velocity, $p$ is the fluid's pressure given by an equation of state (whose choice depends on the nature of the fluid, see below), $g$ is the Minkowski metric\footnote{This problem can be studied for a general background metric, but the Minkowski metric already contains all the important mathematical features.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}u^\mu \partial_\mu \varrho + (p+\varrho) \partial_\mu u^\mu = 0, \label{E:Energy_conservation} (p+\varrho) u^\mu \partial_\mu u^\alpha + \proj^{\alpha\mu} \partial_\mu p =0, \label{E:Momentum_conservation} g_{\mu\nu} u^\mu u^\nu = -1, \label{E:Constraint_four_velocity}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-008: Coupling to Einstein's equations, on the other hand, is an entirely different (and much harder) problem.
elements.sent-2411-009: Above and throughout, we employ standard Cartesian coordinates $\{ x^\alpha \}_{\alpha=0 ^3$ with $t:= x^0$ denoting a time coordinate and $x := (x^1,x^2,x^3)$ denoting spatial coordinates, the sum convention is adopted, Greek indices vary from $0$ to $3$ and Latin indices from $1$ to $3$, and indices are raised and lowered with $g$.}
elements.sent-2411-010: We note that \eqref{E:Energy_conservation is the conservation of energy for the fluid, \eqref{E:Momentum_conservation is the conservation of momentum, and \eqref{E:Constraint_four_velocity is a normalization condition where the velocity is assumed to be a forward time-like vector field (and this constraint is propagated by the flow).}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: We are interested in the case where the fluid is confined within a domain that is not fixed but is allowed to move with the fluid motion, such as, e.g., the motion of a star.
elements.sent-2411-012: Fluids of this type are called free-boundary fluids.
elements.sent-2411-013: We further consider the situation where the pressure and density vanish on the boundary which is often referred to as the gas case.
elements.sent-2411-014: The liquid case where the density does not vanish on the boundary is a different problem.
elements.sent-2411-015: Denote the region containing the fluid at time $t$ by $\Omega_t$.
elements.sent-2411-016: Then, $\Omega_t$ can be described as \begin{equation}\Omega_t = \{ (\tau,x) \in R^{1+3} \, | \, \tau = t, \varrho(t,x) > 0 \}. \nonumber\end{equation} Equations \eqref{E:Euler hold in the spacetime region \begin{equation}\mathscr{D} := \bigcup_{0\leq t < T} \{ t \} \times \Omega_t, \label{E:moving_domain_definition}\end{equation} for some $T>0$, known as the moving domain.}
elements.sent-2411-017: The fluid's free-boundary at time $t$ is defined as $\Gamma_t : = \partial \Omega_t$, and the fluid's free boundary, also called the moving boundary, free interface, or vacuum boundary, is defined as \begin{equation}\Gamma : = \bigcup_{0\leq t < T} \{ t \} \times \Gamma_t = \bigcup_{0\leq t < T} \{ t \} \times \partial \Omega_t. \nonumber\end{equation} Sometimes we also call $\Omega_t$ and $\Gamma_t$ the moving domain and the free boundary, respectively.
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}\Gamma : = \bigcup_{0\leq t < T} \{ t \} \times \Gamma_t = \bigcup_{0\leq t < T} \{ t \} \times \partial \Omega_t. \nonumber\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-018: In free-boundary problems, understanding the dynamics of $\Gamma$ is crucial, as it is the fact that $\Gamma_t$ moves with time that distinguishes such problems from a standard initial-boundary value problem where the boundary of the domain is fixed.
elements.sent-2411-019: Note that, according to the foregoing, we have \begin{equation}p = \varrho = 0 \, \text{ on } \, \Gamma. \label{E:Pressure_and_density_vanish_boundary}\end{equation}.
elements.subsection-2411-001: Physical vacuum boundary with a barotropic equation of state\label{S:Physical_vacuum_barotropic}
elements.sent-2411-020: Before discussing our work for a non-barotropic equation of state, let us consider the barotropic setting which will motivate our discussion.
elements.sent-2411-021: Consider the equation of state: \begin{equation}p = p(\varrho) = \varrho^{\kappa+1}, \kappa > 0, \label{E:Equation_state_barotropic}\end{equation} where $\kappa$ is constant.
elements.sent-2411-022: Equation \eqref{E:Momentum_conservation then becomes \begin{equation}(\varrho^{\kappa+1}+\varrho) u^\mu \partial_\mu u^\alpha + c_s^2 \proj^{\alpha\mu} \partial_\mu \varrho =0, \label{E:Momentum_conservation_barotropic}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}c_s^2 := p^\prime(\varrho) = (\kappa+1)\varrho^\kappa \label{E:Sound_speed_barotropic}\end{equation} is the fluid's sound speed \cite{disconzi2023recent and the second equality in \eqref{E:Sound_speed_barotropic is valid for the equation of state \eqref{E:Equation_state_barotropic .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Since $\varrho$ vanishes on the free-boundary, we also have \begin{equation}\left. c_s^2 \right|_{\Gamma} = 0. \label{E:Sound_speed_vansih_boundary}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-024: Observe that \eqref{E:Sound_speed_vansih_boundary follows immediately from \eqref{E:Pressure_and_density_vanish_boundary and \eqref{E:Sound_speed_barotropic .}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: But typically one would still impose \eqref{E:Sound_speed_vansih_boundary for other equations of state than \eqref{E:Equation_state_barotropic when \eqref{E:Pressure_and_density_vanish_boundary holds, since sound waves should not be allowed to propagate into vacuum across a region where the medium (the density) vanishes.}}}
elements.sent-2411-026: The decay rate of $c_s^2$ near the free-boundary plays a key role in this problem.
elements.sent-2411-027: One needs to consider decay rates that allow for $\Gamma_t$ to move with a bounded non-zero acceleration.
elements.sent-2411-028: We have from \eqref{E:Momentum_conservation_barotropic and \eqref{E:Sound_speed_barotropic that the fluid's (four-)acceleration is \begin{equation}a^\alpha := u^\mu \partial_\mu u^\alpha = -\frac{ (\kappa+1)\varrho^{\kappa-1}}{1+\varrho^\kappa} \proj^{\alpha\mu} \partial_\mu \varrho. \nonumber\end{equation} Since $\varrho \sim 0$ near the free-boundary, we have that $1+\varrho = O(1)$ so, near the boundary, \begin{equation}a^\alpha \sim \varrho^{\kappa-1} \proj^{\alpha\mu} \partial_\mu \varrho \sim \partial \varrho^\kappa \sim \partial c_s^2, \nonumber\end{equation} using $\bm{\partial}$ to denote a generic spacetime derivative.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: At this point, we need to make some assumption on the decay rate of $c_s^2$.
elements.sent-2411-030: In view of the finite speed propagation property, away from the boundary the motion of the fluid is essentially the same as in the case without a free-boundary.
elements.sent-2411-031: Thus, a natural scale in this problem which allows us to separate the bulk and boundary behaviors is the distance to the boundary: \begin{equation}d \equiv d(t,x) = \, \text {distance from } \, x \,  to  \, \Gamma_t. \nonumber\end{equation} Therefore, a natural assumption to make on $c_s^2$ is that it decays like a power of $d$, i.e., $c_s^2 \sim d^\beta$ for some $\beta >0$.
elements.sent-2411-032: Under this assumption, the acceleration becomes \begin{equation}a \sim \partial d^\beta = \beta d^{\beta-1} \partial d \sim d^{\beta-1}, \nonumber\end{equation} where we used that $\bm{\partial d = O(1)$.}
elements.sent-2411-033: From this, we see that \begin{equation}\left. a \right|_{\Gamma_t} =  0, \beta > 1, \infty, 0<\beta < 1, finite\neq 0, \beta = 1.  \nonumber\end{equation} Hence, we expect a realistic dynamics only in the case $\beta=1$.
elements.sent-2411-034: Therefore, we assume that \begin{equation}c_s^2(t,x) \approx d(t,x) \,  for \, x\,  near  \, \Gamma_t, \label{E:Physical_boundary}\end{equation} i.e., $c_s^2$ is comparable to the distance to the boundary.
elements.sent-2411-035: Condition \eqref{E:Physical_boundary is known as the physical vacuum boundary condition.}
elements.sent-2411-036: One should understand \eqref{E:Physical_boundary as a constraint, i.e., something that is imposed on the initial data and then propagated by the flow.}
elements.sent-2411-037: The local well-posedness of the relativistic Euler equations with \eqref{E:Equation_state_barotropic as equation of state and initial data satisfying the physical vacuum boundary condition was obtained in \cite{DisconziIfrimTataru using Eulerian coordinates.}}
elements.sent-2411-038: Additionally, a-priori estimates for the same problem with equation of state \eqref{E:Equation_state_barotropic were obtained in \cite{Hadzic-Shkoller-Speck-2019 using Lagrangian coordinates.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: In \cite{Jang-LeFloch-Masmoudi-2016 , the authors proved a priori estimates using an equation of state where the pressure is assumed to be a power law of the fluid's baryon density $n$ (see \eqref{E:Baryon_density_conservation below).}}
elements.subsection-2411-002: The case of an ideal gas
elements.sent-2411-040: We would like to extend the results of \cite{DisconziIfrimTataru to the case of a non-barotropic equation of state.}
elements.sent-2411-041: In addition, we would like, if possible, to treat situations that are of direct relevance to physicists working in numerical simulations of star evolution.
elements.sent-2411-042: A typical equation of state used by physicists in their simulations of stars is that of an ideal gas (see \cite{RezzollaZanottiBookRelHydro-2013 Section 2.4), \begin{equation}p = p(n,\varepsilon) = n \varepsilon (\gamma -1 ), \label{E:Equation_state_ideal_gas}\end{equation} where $n$ is the fluid's baryon density, $\varepsilon$ is the fluid's internal energy, and $\gamma >1$ is a constant.}
elements.sent-2411-043: We recall that $n$ satisfies the equation \begin{equation}u^\mu\partial_\mu n + n \partial_\mu u^\mu = 0, \label{E:Baryon_density_conservation}\end{equation} and $\varepsilon$ is defined through the relation \begin{equation}\varrho = n(1 + \varepsilon). \label{E:Definition_internal_energy}\end{equation} Equation \eqref{E:Baryon_density_conservation is known as the conservation of baryon density \cite{disconzi2023recent .}}
elements.sent-2411-044: This provides a closed system in terms of the unknowns $(\varepsilon, n, u^\mu)$.
elements.sent-2411-045: Since $\varepsilon\geq 0$, equation \eqref{E:Definition_internal_energy implies that $n$ also vanishes on the free boundary.}
elements.sent-2411-046: In addition, since we must have $\varepsilon = 0$ in a vacuum, we also need to impose that $\varepsilon = 0$ on $\Gamma_t$.
elements.sent-2411-047: We need to find conditions that play a similar role to the physical vacuum boundary condition \eqref{E:Physical_boundary employed for barotropic fluids.}
elements.sent-2411-048: As in Section \ref{S:Physical_vacuum_barotropic , we try to determine such conditions by ensuring a finite non-zero boundary acceleration.}
elements.sent-2411-049: We now adopt $(\varepsilon, n, u)$ as primitive variables.

elements.subsection-2411-004: The linearized system in terms of entropy and sound speed (squared)
elements.sent-2411-073: Our goal is to rewrite \eqref{E:Euler_ideal_gas so that we can apply energy estimate techniques.}
elements.sent-2411-074: For our purposes, it is more effective to consider the unknowns $(s,r,u^\mu)$ where $s$ denotes the entropy per particle, $r$ is a multiple of the sound speed (squared), and $u$ is the usual (four)-velocity.
elements.sent-2411-075: We will often abuse language and simply refer to $r$ as the sound speed (squared) or just the sound speed.
elements.sent-2411-076: We begin by rewriting \eqref{E:Euler_ideal_gas in terms of $p$, and then introducing our new variables which will be used in the remainder of the work.}
elements.sent-2411-077: First, using \eqref{E:Equation_state_ideal_gas and \eqref{E:Internal_energy_conservation_ideal_gas , we observe that \begin{equation}\begin{split} u^\mu \partial_\mu p = (\gamma-1)n u^\mu \partial_\mu \varepsilon + (\gamma-1) \varepsilon u^\mu \partial_\mu n = -(\gamma-1)^2 n \varepsilon \partial_\mu u^\mu - (\gamma-1)n \varepsilon \partial_\mu u^\mu = - \gamma p \partial_\mu u^\mu. \end{split}\end{equation} Then, rewriting \eqref{E:Momentum_conservation_ideal_gas in terms of $\varepsilon$ and $p$, our system \eqref{E:Euler_ideal_gas takes the form \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\label{E:New_eq_1} u^\mu \partial_\mu \varepsilon + (\gamma-1)\varepsilon \partial_\mu u^\mu =0 \label{E:New_eq_2} \frac{1+\varepsilon \gamma}{\gamma-1} p u^\mu \partial_\mu u^\alpha + \varepsilon \proj^{\alpha \mu} \partial_\mu p = 0 \label{E:New_eq_3} u^\mu \partial_\mu p +\gamma p \partial_\mu u^\mu =0.\end{equation}.}}}}}

elements.formula-2411-030: \begin{equation}\label{E:New_eq_1} u^\mu \partial_\mu \varepsilon + (\gamma-1)\varepsilon \partial_\mu u^\mu =0 \label{E:New_eq_2} \frac{1+\varepsilon \gamma}{\gamma-1} p u^\mu \partial_\mu u^\alpha + \varepsilon \proj^{\alpha \mu} \partial_\mu p = 0 \label{E:New_eq_3} u^\mu \partial_\mu p +\gamma p \partial_\mu u^\mu =0.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-078: Recall that the enthalpy per particle $h$ is defined by \begin{equation}\label{E:enthalpy_definition} h = \frac{p + \varrho }{n} = \frac{n \varepsilon(\gamma-1) + n(1+\varepsilon))}{n} = \varepsilon\gamma +1\end{equation} which has been simplified using \eqref{E:Equation_state_ideal_gas and \eqref{E:Definition_internal_energy .}}
elements.sent-2411-079: Using the well known thermodynamic relation \begin{equation}\label{E:thermodynamic_relations} n dh - dp = n \theta ds,\end{equation} where $\theta$ is the temperature for the fluid, and the fact that \begin{equation}\label{E:p=ntheta} p = n \theta\end{equation} for an ideal fluid, we can solve for $s$ by \begin{equation}\label{E:s_simplification} \begin{split} n \gamma d\varepsilon - (\gamma-1)(n d\varepsilon +\varepsilon dn) = n \varepsilon (\gamma-1) ds \implies s = \frac{1}{\gamma-1} \log\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{n^{\gamma-1}} \right) + s_0 \end{split}\end{equation} where $s_0$ is a constant (and we will often set $s_0 =0$ for convenience).

elements.sent-2411-402: This gives \begin{equation}\begin{split} \left|\partial^l \hat{\omega}\right|_G^2 \gtrsim \left|\partial^l \hat{\omega}\right|_{\delta^{(4)}}^2 = \delta^{\alpha \gamma} \delta^{\beta \delta}\partial^l \hat{\omega}_{\alpha \beta} \partial^l \hat{\omega}_{\gamma \delta} = \delta^{i \gamma} \delta^{\beta \delta}\partial^l \hat{\omega}_{i \beta} \partial^l \hat{\omega}_{\gamma \delta} + \delta^{0 \gamma} \delta^{\beta \delta}\partial^l \hat{\omega}_{0 \beta} \partial^l \hat{\omega}_{\gamma \delta} = \delta^{i j} \delta^{\beta \delta}\partial^l \hat{\omega}_{i \beta} \partial^l \hat{\omega}_{j \delta} + \textcolor{red}{\delta^{i 0} \delta^{\beta \delta}\partial^l \hat{\omega}_{i \beta} \partial^l \hat{\omega}_{0 \delta}} + \delta^{\beta \delta}\partial^l \hat{\omega}_{0 \beta} \partial^l \hat{\omega}_{0 \delta} = \delta^{i j} \delta^{k \delta}\partial^l \hat{\omega}_{i k} \partial^l \hat{\omega}_{j \delta} + \delta^{i j} \delta^{0 \delta}\partial^l \hat{\omega}_{i 0} \partial^l \hat{\omega}_{j \delta} + \delta^{\beta \delta}\partial^l \hat{\omega}_{0 \beta} \partial^l \hat{\omega}_{0 \delta} = \delta^{i j} \delta^{k m}\partial^l \hat{\omega}_{i k} \partial^l \hat{\omega}_{j m} + \textcolor{red}{ \delta^{i j} \delta^{k 0}\partial^l \hat{\omega}_{i k} \partial^l \hat{\omega}_{j 0}} + \delta^{i j} \partial^l \hat{\omega}_{i 0} \partial^l \hat{\omega}_{j 0} + \textcolor{purple}{\delta^{\beta \delta}\partial^l \hat{\omega}_{0 \beta} \partial^l \hat{\omega}_{0 \delta}} = \left| \partial^l \overrightarrow{\hat{\omega}}\right|_{\delta^{(3)}}^2 + \delta^{i j} \partial^l \hat{\omega}_{i 0} \partial^l \hat{\omega}_{j 0} + \delta^{ij}\partial^l \hat{\omega}_{0 i} \partial^l \hat{\omega}_{0 j} \end{split}\end{equation} where we split indices, the \textcolor{red {red terms are zero, and the \textcolor{purple {purple term is zero when $\beta=0$ or $\delta=0$ using the definition of $\hat{\omega}$.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-568: After observing $D_t^{2k h^\alpha$, we see that it contains terms of the form $D_t^{2k (\tilde{u , \tilde{s , \tilde{r )$ which are order $-\frac{1 {2}$ supercritical at worst.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-569: However, those terms all get multiplied by $r D_t^{2k \tilde{u}$ which has order $\frac{1 {2}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-570: Thus, by Lemma \ref{L:Sum_orders , these terms are also estimated using the $\mathcal{H ^{2k}$ norm and we can solve for any expressions like $D_t^{2k-i (\proj^{\alpha \mu \partial_\mu r)$ in terms of spatial derivatives of $u, r,$ and $s$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-571: For the second term in \eqref{E:higher_order_Wave_estimates_2 , we get the following: \begin{equation}(\Gamma +r) r G_{\alpha \beta} D_t^{2k} \tilde{u}^\beta \sum_{i=0}^{2k-1} D_t^{2k-i} \left(\frac{1}{\Gamma+r} \proj^{\alpha \mu} \right) \partial_\mu \left(D_t^i \tilde{r}\right)\end{equation} Now, when $i=2k-1$, $D_t^{2k-1 \tilde{r}$ contains terms that have order $\frac{1 {2}$ at worst by Remarks \ref{D:definition_critical_subcritical_supercritical and \ref{R:Order_of_operators .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-572: This implies that $\partial (D_t^{2k-1 \tilde{r )$ will contain terms that are order $-\frac{1 {2}$ at worst by Lemma \ref{L:Order_of_operators .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-573: However, the multiplier $rD_t^{2k \tilde{u}$has order $\frac{1 {2}$, which is just enough to allow us to apply Lemma \ref{L:Sum_orders .}}}
elements.sent-2411-574: Thus, we can estimate this term using the $\mathcal{H ^{2k}$ norm.}
elements.sent-2411-575: For the third and final term in \eqref{E:higher_order_Wave_estimates_2 , we can follow the analysis of the second term and use Lemma \ref{L:Commutator to get \begin{equation}\begin{split} (\Gamma +r) r G_{\alpha \beta} D_t^{2k} \tilde{u}^\beta \sum_{i=1}^{2k} D_t^{2k-i} \left(\frac{1}{\Gamma+r} \proj^{\alpha \mu} \right) [\partial_\mu , D_t^i] \tilde{r} \simeq (\Gamma +r) G_{\alpha \beta} r D_t^{2k} \tilde{u}^\beta \sum_{i=1}^{2k} D_t^{2k-i} \left(\frac{1}{\Gamma+r} \proj^{\alpha \mu} \right) \left(\sum_{j=0}^{i-1} C^\nu_j \partial_\nu (D_t^j \tilde{r}) \right) \end{split}\end{equation} Since the worst terms appear when $i=2k$, we see that once again $D_t^{2k-1 \tilde{r}$ has terms with order $\frac{1 {2}$ at worst, thus, $\partial (D_t^{2k-1 \tilde{r )$ has terms with $-\frac{1 {2}$ order.}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-271: \begin{equation} (\Gamma +r) r G_{\alpha \beta} D_t^{2k} \tilde{u}^\beta \sum_{i=1}^{2k} D_t^{2k-i} \left(\frac{1}{\Gamma+r} \proj^{\alpha \mu} \right) [\partial_\mu , D_t^i] \tilde{r} \simeq (\Gamma +r) G_{\alpha \beta} r D_t^{2k} \tilde{u}^\beta \sum_{i=1}^{2k} D_t^{2k-i} \left(\frac{1}{\Gamma+r} \proj^{\alpha \mu} \right) \left(\sum_{j=0}^{i-1} C^\nu_j \partial_\nu (D_t^j \tilde{r}) \right) \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-576: However, the multiplier has order $\frac{1 {2}$, and this allows us to apply Lemma $\ref{L:Sum_orders}$ as usual.}
elements.sent-2411-577: This completes estimate \eqref{E:higher_order_Wave_estimates_2 , and all higher order terms on the RHS in \eqref{E:Linearized_System_6 have been handled.}}
elements.sent-2411-578: Combining the previous sections, we have reached our final result:.
elements.sent-2411-579: Combining Proposition \ref{P:Entropy Estimates and Theorem \ref{Th:Higher_order_energy_estimates , we get the following \begin{equation}\frac{d}{dt} \left(\norm{\tilde{s}}^2_{H^{2k, k + \frac{2-\gamma}{2(\gamma-1)}+ \frac{1}{2} }}+ E^{2k}_{\text{wave}}(\tilde{s}, \tilde{r}, \tilde{u}) \right) \lesssim \mathcal{C}_1 \norm{(\tilde{s}, \tilde{r}, \tilde{u})}^2_{\mathcal{H}^{2k}} .\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-583: Here, we record some of the computations for proving Lemma \ref{L:Solving_for_time_derivatives .}
elements.sent-2411-584: Using \eqref{E:System directly, we have \begin{equation}\label{E:start_solving_for_time} \begin{split} \partial_t s = - \frac{u^i}{u^0} \partial_i s \partial_t r = - \frac{u^i}{u^0} \partial_i r - \frac{\gamma-1}{u^0} r \partial_t u^0 - \frac{\gamma-1}{u^0} r \partial_i u^i \partial_t u^\alpha = - \frac{u^i}{u^0} \partial_i u^\alpha - \frac{1}{(\Gamma+r)u^0} \proj^{\alpha 0} \partial_t r - \frac{1}{(\Gamma+r)u^0} \proj^{\alpha i} \partial_i r \end{split}\end{equation} Starting from \eqref{E:start_solving_for_time , we can further solve for $\partial_t u^0$ by plugging in $\alpha=0$ into the last equation.}}
elements.formula-2411-277: \begin{equation}\label{E:start_solving_for_time}  \partial_t s = - \frac{u^i}{u^0} \partial_i s \partial_t r = - \frac{u^i}{u^0} \partial_i r - \frac{\gamma-1}{u^0} r \partial_t u^0 - \frac{\gamma-1}{u^0} r \partial_i u^i \partial_t u^\alpha = - \frac{u^i}{u^0} \partial_i u^\alpha - \frac{1}{(\Gamma+r)u^0} \proj^{\alpha 0} \partial_t r - \frac{1}{(\Gamma+r)u^0} \proj^{\alpha i} \partial_i r \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-585: If we set $a_1 = u^0 - \frac{\gamma-1 {(\Gamma+r)u^0 r \proj^{00}$, then \begin{equation}\label{E:partial_t u^0} \begin{split} \partial_t u^0 = \frac{1}{a_1} \left(-u^i \partial_i u^0 + \frac{\gamma-1}{(\Gamma+r)u^0}r \proj^{00} \partial_i u^i + \frac{1}{(\Gamma+r)u^0}\proj^{00} u^i \partial_i r - \frac{1}{\Gamma+r} \proj^{0i} \partial_i r \right) = -\frac{1}{a_1} u^i \partial_i u^0 - \frac{1}{a_1(\Gamma+r)u^0} u^i \partial_i r + \frac{(\gamma-1)((u^0)^2-1)}{a_1(\Gamma+r)u^0}r \partial_i u^i := C_1^i \partial_i u^0 + C_2^i \partial_i r + C_3 r \partial_i u^i \simeq \partial u^0 + \partial r + r \partial u \implies \partial_t \tilde{u}^0 = C_1^i \partial_i \tilde{u}^0+ C_2^i \partial_i \tilde{r} + C_3 r \partial_i \tilde{u}^i + C_3\tilde{r} \partial_i u^i + \widetilde{C_1^i} \partial_i u^0 + \widetilde{C_2^i} \partial_i r + \widetilde{C_3} r \partial_i u^i \end{split}\end{equation} We have the computations \begin{equation}a_1 = u^0 - \frac{(\gamma-1)((u^0)^2-1)}{(\Gamma+r)u^0}r \implies \tilde{a}_1 = \tilde{u}^0 - \frac{(\gamma-1)((u^0)^2-1)}{(\Gamma+r)u^0} \tilde{r} - r \left( \frac{2(\Gamma+r)(\gamma-1)u^0 \tilde{u}^0 -(\gamma-1)((u^0)^2-1)[(\Gamma' \tilde{s} + \tilde{r})u^0 + (\Gamma +r)\tilde{u}^0] }{(\Gamma+r)^2 (u^0)^2} \right)\end{equation} and \begin{equation}C_1^i := -\frac{u^i}{a_1} \implies \widetilde{C_1^i} = \frac{u^i \tilde{a}_1 - a_1 \tilde{u}^i}{(a_1)^2} C_2^i := \frac{1}{(\Gamma+r)u^0} C_1^i \implies \widetilde{C_2^i} = \frac{1}{(\Gamma+r)u^0} \widetilde{C_1^i} - \frac{[(\Gamma' \tilde{s} + \tilde{r})u^0 + (\Gamma +r)\tilde{u}^0]}{(\Gamma+r)^2 (u^0)^2} C_1^i C_3 := \frac{(\gamma-1)((u^0)^2-1)}{u^i} C_2^i \implies \widetilde{C_3} = \frac{(\gamma-1)((u^0)^2-1)}{u^i} \widetilde{C_2^i} + \frac{u^i 2(\gamma-1) \tilde{u}^0 -(\gamma-1)((u^0)^2-1) \tilde{u}^i }{(u^i)^2} C_2^i.\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-278: \begin{equation}\label{E:partial_t u^0}  \partial_t u^0 = \frac{1}{a_1} \left(-u^i \partial_i u^0 + \frac{\gamma-1}{(\Gamma+r)u^0}r \proj^{00} \partial_i u^i + \frac{1}{(\Gamma+r)u^0}\proj^{00} u^i \partial_i r - \frac{1}{\Gamma+r} \proj^{0i} \partial_i r \right) = -\frac{1}{a_1} u^i \partial_i u^0 - \frac{1}{a_1(\Gamma+r)u^0} u^i \partial_i r + \frac{(\gamma-1)((u^0)^2-1)}{a_1(\Gamma+r)u^0}r \partial_i u^i := C_1^i \partial_i u^0 + C_2^i \partial_i r + C_3 r \partial_i u^i \simeq \partial u^0 + \partial r + r \partial u \implies \partial_t \tilde{u}^0 = C_1^i \partial_i \tilde{u}^0+ C_2^i \partial_i \tilde{r} + C_3 r \partial_i \tilde{u}^i + C_3\tilde{r} \partial_i u^i + \widetilde{C_1^i} \partial_i u^0 + \widetilde{C_2^i} \partial_i r + \widetilde{C_3} r \partial_i u^i \end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-280: \begin{equation}C_1^i := -\frac{u^i}{a_1} \implies \widetilde{C_1^i} = \frac{u^i \tilde{a}_1 - a_1 \tilde{u}^i}{(a_1)^2} C_2^i := \frac{1}{(\Gamma+r)u^0} C_1^i \implies \widetilde{C_2^i} = \frac{1}{(\Gamma+r)u^0} \widetilde{C_1^i} - \frac{[(\Gamma' \tilde{s} + \tilde{r})u^0 + (\Gamma +r)\tilde{u}^0]}{(\Gamma+r)^2 (u^0)^2} C_1^i C_3 := \frac{(\gamma-1)((u^0)^2-1)}{u^i} C_2^i \implies \widetilde{C_3} = \frac{(\gamma-1)((u^0)^2-1)}{u^i} \widetilde{C_2^i} + \frac{u^i 2(\gamma-1) \tilde{u}^0 -(\gamma-1)((u^0)^2-1) \tilde{u}^i }{(u^i)^2} C_2^i.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-586: Note that after analyzing all of the terms (with $k=1$ here), we see that a large number are subcritical, and we can simplify: \begin{equation}\partial_t \tilde{u}^0 \simeq \partial \tilde{u}^0 + \partial \tilde{r} + r \partial \tilde{u} + \tilde{r} \partial u \simeq ( \text{$H^2$ super-critical of order $-\frac{1}{2}$ }) + ($H^2$ critical) + (\text{$H^2$ sub-critical of order $\frac{1}{2}$})\end{equation} Similiary, we can simplify $\partial_t r$ and $\partial_t \tilde{r}$ in the following way: \begin{equation}\begin{split} \partial_t r = - \frac{u^i}{u^0} \partial_i r - \frac{\gamma-1}{u^0}r \partial_i u^i - \frac{\gamma-1}{ u^0} r \left(C_1^i \partial_i u^0 + C_2^i \partial_i r + C_3 r \partial_i u^i \right) \implies \partial_t \tilde{r} = - \frac{u^i}{u^0} \partial_i \tilde{r} - \frac{\gamma-1}{u^0}r \partial_i \tilde{u}^i - \frac{\gamma-1}{ u^0} r \left(\partial_t \tilde{u}^0 \right) + \frac{u^i \tilde{u}^0 - u^0 \tilde{u}^i }{(u^0)^2} \partial_i r + \left(\frac{(\gamma-1)}{(u^0)^2} \tilde{u}^0 r - \frac{\gamma-1}{u^0} \tilde{r}\right) \partial_i u^i + \left(\frac{(\gamma-1)}{(u^0)^2} \tilde{u}^0 r - \frac{\gamma-1}{u^0} \tilde{r}\right) \partial_t u^0 \simeq \partial \tilde{r} + r \partial \tilde{u} + r \partial \tilde{u}^0 + r \partial \tilde{r} + r^2 \partial \tilde{u} + (\text{additional subcritical terms}) \partial_t u^j = - \frac{1}{(\Gamma+r)u^0} \proj^{j 0} \left[\partial_t r\right]- \frac{u^i}{u^0} \partial_i u^j - \frac{1}{(\Gamma+r)u^0} \proj^{j i} \partial_i r \simeq -[\partial_t r] - \partial u - \partial r \end{split}\end{equation} The expressions for $\partial_t s$ and $\partial_t \tilde{s}$ take the easiest form, as \begin{equation}\begin{split} \partial_t s = - \frac{u^i}{u^0} \partial_i s \partial_t \tilde{s} = - \frac{u^i}{u^0} \partial_i \tilde{s} + \frac{u^i \tilde{u}^0 - u^0 \tilde{u}^i }{(u^0)^2} \partial_i s \end{split}\end{equation} We also see that $(u^0)^2 = 1 + u^j u_j$, so \begin{equation}\label{E:solve_for_partial_i_u^0} \begin{split} \tilde{u}^0 = \frac{u_j}{u^0} \tilde{u}^j \partial_i \tilde{u}^0 = \frac{u_j}{u^0} \partial_i \tilde{u}^j + \tilde{u}^j \partial_i \frac{u_j}{u_0} \end{split}\end{equation} Thus, linearizing our expression for $\partial_t u^j$, we get \begin{equation}\partial_t \tilde{u}^j \simeq [\partial_t \tilde{r}] + \partial \tilde{u} + \partial \tilde{r} \simeq \partial \tilde{u} + \partial \tilde{r} + (r \partial \tilde{u} + r \partial \tilde{r} + r^2 \partial \tilde{u} + (additional subcritical terms))\end{equation} Starting from \eqref{E:start_solving_for_time , we can further solve for $\partial_t u^0$ by plugging in $\alpha=0$ into the last equation.}

elements.formula-2411-282: \begin{equation} \partial_t r = - \frac{u^i}{u^0} \partial_i r - \frac{\gamma-1}{u^0}r \partial_i u^i - \frac{\gamma-1}{ u^0} r \left(C_1^i \partial_i u^0 + C_2^i \partial_i r + C_3 r \partial_i u^i \right) \implies \partial_t \tilde{r} = - \frac{u^i}{u^0} \partial_i \tilde{r} - \frac{\gamma-1}{u^0}r \partial_i \tilde{u}^i - \frac{\gamma-1}{ u^0} r \left(\partial_t \tilde{u}^0 \right) + \frac{u^i \tilde{u}^0 - u^0 \tilde{u}^i }{(u^0)^2} \partial_i r + \left(\frac{(\gamma-1)}{(u^0)^2} \tilde{u}^0 r - \frac{\gamma-1}{u^0} \tilde{r}\right) \partial_i u^i + \left(\frac{(\gamma-1)}{(u^0)^2} \tilde{u}^0 r - \frac{\gamma-1}{u^0} \tilde{r}\right) \partial_t u^0 \simeq \partial \tilde{r} + r \partial \tilde{u} + r \partial \tilde{u}^0 + r \partial \tilde{r} + r^2 \partial \tilde{u} + (additional subcritical terms) \partial_t u^j = - \frac{1}{(\Gamma+r)u^0} \proj^{j 0} \left[\partial_t r\right]- \frac{u^i}{u^0} \partial_i u^j - \frac{1}{(\Gamma+r)u^0} \proj^{j i} \partial_i r \simeq -[\partial_t r] - \partial u - \partial r \end{equation}

\frac{\gamma-1}{u^0} \tilde{r}\right) \partial_i u^i + \left(\frac{(\gamma-1)}{(u^0)^2} \tilde{u}^0 r - \frac{\gamma-1}{u^0} \tilde{r}\right) \partial_t u^0 \simeq \partial \tilde{r} + r \partial \tilde{u} + r \partial \tilde{u}^0 + r \partial \tilde{r} + r^2 \partial \tilde{u} + (\text{additional subcritical terms}) \partial_t u^j = - \frac{1}{(\Gamma+r)u^0} \proj^{j 0} \left[\partial_t r\right]- \frac{u^i}{u^0} \partial_i u^j - \frac{1}{(\Gamma+r)u^0} \proj^{j i} \partial_i r \simeq -[\partial_t r] - \partial u - \partial r \end{split}\end{equation} The expressions for $\partial_t s$ and $\partial_t \tilde{s}$ take the easiest form, as \begin{equation}\begin{split} \partial_t s = - \frac{u^i}{u^0} \partial_i s \partial_t \tilde{s} = - \frac{u^i}{u^0} \partial_i \tilde{s} + \frac{u^i \tilde{u}^0 - u^0 \tilde{u}^i }{(u^0)^2} \partial_i s \end{split}\end{equation} We also see that $(u^0)^2 = 1 + u^j u_j$, so \begin{equation}\begin{split} \tilde{u}^0 = \frac{u_j}{u^0} \tilde{u}^j \partial_i \tilde{u}^0 = \frac{u_j}{u^0} \partial_i \tilde{u}^j + \tilde{u}^j \partial_i \frac{u_j}{u_0} \end{split}\end{equation} Thus, linearizing our expression for $\partial_t u^j$, we get \begin{equation}\partial_t \tilde{u}^j \simeq [\partial_t \tilde{r}] + \partial \tilde{u} + \partial \tilde{r} \simeq \partial \tilde{u} + \partial \tilde{r} + (r \partial \tilde{u} + r \partial \tilde{r} + r^2 \partial \tilde{u} + (additional subcritical terms))\end{equation} \bibliographystyle{plain.}
